name: task-manager
description: Use when work feels overwhelming or disorganized. Example: 'Help me prioritize my tasks for this week'. Delivers a prioritized task list with scheduling, context tags, and review cadence.
author: Claude Code
anti_patterns: Inbox overflow - capturing without processing creates anxiety
Priority inflation - everything 'urgent' means nothing is
Context switching - mixing deep work with admin kills flow
No review cycle - stale tasks accumulate and mislead
Tool obsession - perfect system > consistent usage
short_circuits: Full GTD: Capture → Clarify → Organize → Reflect → Engage
Daily triage: Inbox zero + top 3 priorities
Crisis mode: Only urgent/important, defer rest
quality_loop: All inputs captured to trusted inbox
Each item clarified: actionable? project? reference? trash?
Projects have defined outcomes and next actions
Weekly review completed (clean up, update, plan)

related_skills.when: Protecting deep work blocks for high-priority tasks

references.purpose: Getting Things Done methodology adapted for knowledge work
references.sections: Five stages, horizons of focus, weekly review, context lists

references.purpose: Eisenhower, RICE, WSJF, and MoSCoW comparison with use cases
references.sections: Framework selection, scoring templates, team alignment

references.purpose: Frictionless capture from email, Slack, browser, voice, meeting notes
references.sections: Quick capture tools, parsing rules, inbox processing routines

references.purpose: Calendar-based execution strategy for priority work
references.sections: Block types, buffer management, energy matching, interruption handling

references.purpose: Complete weekly review procedure with templates
references.sections: Inbox zero, project review, calendar review, next week planning, metrics
